titleHtml: Unit 4: The formation of the universe can be explained by the big bang theory
title: Unit 4: The formation of the universe can be explained by the big bang theory

chapters.titleHtml: Chapter 4.1: What is the universe, and how do we make sense of it?
chapters.title: Chapter 4.1: What is the universe, and how do we make sense of it?

concepts.titleHtml: 4.1 Concept 1: “Universe” is understood as different things to different people — p.294–296
concepts.title: 4.1 Concept 1: “Universe” is understood as different things to different people — p.294–296

kps.title: KP1: Universe
kps.coreHtml: The universe is all that exists everywhere, including matter, energy, planets, stars, galaxies, and the space containing them.
kps.core: The universe is all that exists everywhere, including matter, energy, planets, stars, galaxies, and the space containing them.
kps.applicationHtml: Use the term universe to describe the largest physical system being considered.
kps.application: Use the term universe to describe the largest physical system being considered.

kps.title: KP2: Universe as a story
kps.coreHtml: Scientific explanations of the universe can be understood as stories built from observations, evidence, hypotheses, and theories.
kps.core: Scientific explanations of the universe can be understood as stories built from observations, evidence, hypotheses, and theories.
kps.applicationHtml: Connect sky observations to scientific and cultural ways of explaining the universe.
kps.application: Connect sky observations to scientific and cultural ways of explaining the universe.

kps.title: KP3: Beginning of the universe
kps.coreHtml: A scientific story of the universe begins about 13.8 billion years ago.
kps.core: A scientific story of the universe begins about 13.8 billion years ago.
kps.applicationHtml: Recognize the big bang time scale as far beyond ordinary human experience.
kps.application: Recognize the big bang time scale as far beyond ordinary human experience.

kps.title: KP4: Scale of the universe
kps.coreHtml: The size and age of the universe are difficult to imagine because they involve extremely large numbers.
kps.core: The size and age of the universe are difficult to imagine because they involve extremely large numbers.
kps.applicationHtml: Use scale thinking when describing distances, ages, galaxies, and cosmic events.
kps.application: Use scale thinking when describing distances, ages, galaxies, and cosmic events.

kps.title: KP5: Changing scientific understanding
kps.coreHtml: Scientific understanding of the universe changes over time as evidence and interpretation improve.
kps.core: Scientific understanding of the universe changes over time as evidence and interpretation improve.
kps.applicationHtml: Explain why the Milky Way was once thought to be the whole universe, but is now understood as one galaxy among billions.
kps.application: Explain why the Milky Way was once thought to be the whole universe, but is now understood as one galaxy among billions.

kps.titleHtml: KP6: Relative meaning of “all that exists”
kps.title: KP6: Relative meaning of “all that exists”
kps.coreHtml: The meaning of “all that exists” depends on what people know, where they live, and when they live.
kps.core: The meaning of “all that exists” depends on what people know, where they live, and when they live.
kps.applicationHtml: Compare how different people may define the universe based on different experiences and knowledge.
kps.application: Compare how different people may define the universe based on different experiences and knowledge.

kps.title: KP7: Place and perspective
kps.coreHtml: A person’s sense of place can shape what they understand as their world or universe.
kps.core: A person’s sense of place can shape what they understand as their world or universe.
kps.applicationHtml: Use the Ngorongoro Crater example to explain how limited or expanded perspective can change understanding.
kps.application: Use the Ngorongoro Crater example to explain how limited or expanded perspective can change understanding.

concepts.titleHtml: 4.1 Concept 2: The sense we make of the universe depends on who we are, when we are, and what we know — p.297
concepts.title: 4.1 Concept 2: The sense we make of the universe depends on who we are, when we are, and what we know — p.297

kps.title: KP1: Context shapes understanding
kps.coreHtml: A person’s understanding of the universe is shaped by identity, time period, place, knowledge, and experience.
kps.core: A person’s understanding of the universe is shaped by identity, time period, place, knowledge, and experience.
kps.applicationHtml: Explain why people in different cultures or historical periods may interpret the universe differently.
kps.application: Explain why people in different cultures or historical periods may interpret the universe differently.

kps.title: KP2: Technology and worldview
kps.coreHtml: Technologies available in a time period affect how people communicate, observe, and understand the world.
kps.core: Technologies available in a time period affect how people communicate, observe, and understand the world.
kps.applicationHtml: Compare communication technologies from different eras to show how knowledge and daily life change.
kps.application: Compare communication technologies from different eras to show how knowledge and daily life change.

kps.title: KP3: Culture shock
kps.coreHtml: Culture shock is anxiety or disorientation caused by entering a dramatically new social or cultural situation.
kps.core: Culture shock is anxiety or disorientation caused by entering a dramatically new social or cultural situation.
kps.applicationHtml: Use culture shock to explain why people from different times or places may misunderstand each other’s knowledge and assumptions.
kps.application: Use culture shock to explain why people from different times or places may misunderstand each other’s knowledge and assumptions.

kps.title: KP4: Time and place
kps.coreHtml: When and where a person lives affect what they think, believe, and understand.
kps.core: When and where a person lives affect what they think, believe, and understand.
kps.applicationHtml: Reflect on how historical period and location influence explanations of the universe.
kps.application: Reflect on how historical period and location influence explanations of the universe.

kps.title: KP5: Assumptions and bias
kps.coreHtml: Ideas about the universe can be influenced by assumptions, bias, and limited perspective.
kps.core: Ideas about the universe can be influenced by assumptions, bias, and limited perspective.
kps.applicationHtml: Question whether a view of the universe is based on evidence, tradition, culture, or personal experience.
kps.application: Question whether a view of the universe is based on evidence, tradition, culture, or personal experience.

kps.title: KP6: Multiple ways of knowing
kps.coreHtml: Scientific understanding can be considered alongside First Peoples perspectives, local knowledge, and other ways of knowing.
kps.core: Scientific understanding can be considered alongside First Peoples perspectives, local knowledge, and other ways of knowing.
kps.applicationHtml: Compare different knowledge systems respectfully when exploring the universe and human place within it.
kps.application: Compare different knowledge systems respectfully when exploring the universe and human place within it.

concepts.titleHtml: Optional Extension — Make a Difference: Take Action — What should we do about light pollution? — p.298
concepts.title: Optional Extension — Make a Difference: Take Action — What should we do about light pollution? — p.298

kps.title: KP1: Light pollution
kps.coreHtml: Light pollution is artificial night lighting that prevents many people from seeing stars and the Milky Way.
kps.core: Light pollution is artificial night lighting that prevents many people from seeing stars and the Milky Way.
kps.applicationHtml: Connect urban lighting to reduced sky visibility.
kps.application: Connect urban lighting to reduced sky visibility.

kps.title: KP2: Benefits of night lighting
kps.coreHtml: Night lighting can improve safety and allow people to work, shop, and move after dark.
kps.core: Night lighting can improve safety and allow people to work, shop, and move after dark.
kps.applicationHtml: Weigh the benefits of lighting against environmental and astronomical costs.
kps.application: Weigh the benefits of lighting against environmental and astronomical costs.

kps.title: KP3: Effects on living things
kps.coreHtml: Light pollution can disrupt sleeping, feeding, migration, and breeding cycles.
kps.core: Light pollution can disrupt sleeping, feeding, migration, and breeding cycles.
kps.applicationHtml: Explain how birds and sea turtles can be harmed by artificial light at night.
kps.application: Explain how birds and sea turtles can be harmed by artificial light at night.

kps.title: KP4: Evidence-based response
kps.coreHtml: Responses to light pollution should consider affected groups, proposed solutions, and the effectiveness of those solutions.
kps.core: Responses to light pollution should consider affected groups, proposed solutions, and the effectiveness of those solutions.
kps.applicationHtml: Build an argument about whether and how communities should reduce light pollution.
kps.application: Build an argument about whether and how communities should reduce light pollution.

chapters.titleHtml: Chapter 4.2: What do we know about the universe based on what we can see only with our eyes?
chapters.title: Chapter 4.2: What do we know about the universe based on what we can see only with our eyes?

concepts.titleHtml: 4.2 Concept 1: We see a variety of objects that appear in the sky in mostly predictable ways — p.302–305
concepts.title: 4.2 Concept 1: We see a variety of objects that appear in the sky in mostly predictable ways — p.302–305

kps.title: KP1: Unaided-eye astronomy
kps.coreHtml: Before telescopes, people observed mostly the same sky objects that can still be seen with the unaided eye.
kps.core: Before telescopes, people observed mostly the same sky objects that can still be seen with the unaided eye.
kps.applicationHtml: Distinguish observations possible with eyes alone from observations requiring technology.
kps.application: Distinguish observations possible with eyes alone from observations requiring technology.

kps.title: KP2: The Sun
kps.coreHtml: The Sun is the most obvious daytime sky object and appears to follow a predictable path across the sky.
kps.core: The Sun is the most obvious daytime sky object and appears to follow a predictable path across the sky.
kps.applicationHtml: Use the Sun’s apparent motion to connect sky observations with time, seasons, and direction.
kps.application: Use the Sun’s apparent motion to connect sky observations with time, seasons, and direction.

kps.title: KP3: The Moon
kps.coreHtml: Earth’s Moon is the only moon visible to the unaided eye and changes appearance from night to night.
kps.core: Earth’s Moon is the only moon visible to the unaided eye and changes appearance from night to night.
kps.applicationHtml: Use Moon phases and movement patterns to explain how people observed time cycles.
kps.application: Use Moon phases and movement patterns to explain how people observed time cycles.

kps.title: KP4: Stars
kps.coreHtml: On a clear, dark night, thousands of stars may be visible, differing in brightness, colour, location, and apparent motion.
kps.core: On a clear, dark night, thousands of stars may be visible, differing in brightness, colour, location, and apparent motion.
kps.applicationHtml: Observe star patterns and motion to identify regular sky changes.
kps.application: Observe star patterns and motion to identify regular sky changes.

kps.title: KP5: Visible planets
kps.coreHtml: Mercury, Venus, Mars, Jupiter, and Saturn can be seen without a telescope.
kps.core: Mercury, Venus, Mars, Jupiter, and Saturn can be seen without a telescope.
kps.applicationHtml: Distinguish planets from stars by noticing their wandering motion against background stars.
kps.application: Distinguish planets from stars by noticing their wandering motion against background stars.

kps.titleHtml: KP6: Other visible sky objects and events
kps.title: KP6: Other visible sky objects and events
kps.coreHtml: Comets, meteors, novas, supernovas, and the Milky Way can also be visible without telescopes.
kps.core: Comets, meteors, novas, supernovas, and the Milky Way can also be visible without telescopes.
kps.applicationHtml: Classify visible sky objects as regular, temporary, or unusual events.
kps.application: Classify visible sky objects as regular, temporary, or unusual events.

kps.title: KP7: Place and sky observation
kps.coreHtml: What people see in the sky depends on place, elevation, horizon, season, and local conditions.
kps.core: What people see in the sky depends on place, elevation, horizon, season, and local conditions.
kps.applicationHtml: Compare sky observations from different locations and explain why visible objects may differ.
kps.application: Compare sky observations from different locations and explain why visible objects may differ.

concepts.titleHtml: 4.2 Concept 2: The appearance and motions of the objects we see can be described using a model called the celestial sphere — p.306–312
concepts.title: 4.2 Concept 2: The appearance and motions of the objects we see can be described using a model called the celestial sphere — p.306–312

kps.title: KP1: Constellations
kps.coreHtml: A constellation is a group of stars that forms a pattern in the sky.
kps.core: A constellation is a group of stars that forms a pattern in the sky.
kps.applicationHtml: Compare how different cultures may connect the same stars into different stories and shapes.
kps.application: Compare how different cultures may connect the same stars into different stories and shapes.

kps.title: KP2: Asterisms and pointer stars
kps.coreHtml: An asterism is a smaller star pattern within or across constellations; the Big Dipper is an asterism with pointer stars.
kps.core: An asterism is a smaller star pattern within or across constellations; the Big Dipper is an asterism with pointer stars.
kps.applicationHtml: Use the Big Dipper’s pointer stars to locate Polaris, the North Star.
kps.application: Use the Big Dipper’s pointer stars to locate Polaris, the North Star.

kps.title: KP3: Polaris and navigation
kps.coreHtml: Polaris is close to the North Celestial Pole and appears nearly fixed in the northern sky.
kps.core: Polaris is close to the North Celestial Pole and appears nearly fixed in the northern sky.
kps.applicationHtml: Use Polaris to determine direction in the northern hemisphere.
kps.application: Use Polaris to determine direction in the northern hemisphere.

kps.title: KP4: Celestial sphere
kps.coreHtml: The celestial sphere is an imaginary rotating sphere on which all visible sky objects appear to lie.
kps.core: The celestial sphere is an imaginary rotating sphere on which all visible sky objects appear to lie.
kps.applicationHtml: Use the celestial sphere model to describe apparent sky motion while remembering that Earth rotates.
kps.application: Use the celestial sphere model to describe apparent sky motion while remembering that Earth rotates.

kps.titleHtml: KP5: Celestial equator and celestial poles
kps.title: KP5: Celestial equator and celestial poles
kps.coreHtml: The celestial equator is Earth’s equator projected into space; the celestial poles are where Earth’s axis extends onto the celestial sphere.
kps.core: The celestial equator is Earth’s equator projected into space; the celestial poles are where Earth’s axis extends onto the celestial sphere.
kps.applicationHtml: Explain why some constellations visible from Canada never set, while others never rise above the horizon.
kps.application: Explain why some constellations visible from Canada never set, while others never rise above the horizon.

kps.title: KP6: Ecliptic and planetary motion
kps.coreHtml: The ecliptic is the Sun’s apparent path across the celestial sphere, caused by Earth’s orbit around the Sun.
kps.core: The ecliptic is the Sun’s apparent path across the celestial sphere, caused by Earth’s orbit around the Sun.
kps.applicationHtml: Connect the Sun, Moon, and planets to the same apparent sky path and explain seasonal visibility.
kps.application: Connect the Sun, Moon, and planets to the same apparent sky path and explain seasonal visibility.

kps.title: KP7: Retrograde motion
kps.coreHtml: Retrograde motion is the apparent backward motion of a planet against background stars, caused by Earth passing an outer planet in its orbit.
kps.core: Retrograde motion is the apparent backward motion of a planet against background stars, caused by Earth passing an outer planet in its orbit.
kps.applicationHtml: Explain why Mars can seem to slow, stop, reverse, and then move forward again.
kps.application: Explain why Mars can seem to slow, stop, reverse, and then move forward again.

kps.title: KP8: Azimuth and altitude
kps.coreHtml: Azimuth measures direction along the horizon from north; altitude measures angular height above the horizon.
kps.core: Azimuth measures direction along the horizon from north; altitude measures angular height above the horizon.
kps.applicationHtml: Describe a sky object’s position using observer-based coordinates.
kps.application: Describe a sky object’s position using observer-based coordinates.

kps.title: KP9: Hand-angle estimates
kps.coreHtml: At arm’s length, one finger is about \(1^\circ\), one fist is about \(10^\circ\), and an open hand span is about \(20^\circ\).
kps.core: At arm’s length, one finger is about \(1 \), one fist is about \(10 \), and an open hand span is about \(20 \).
kps.applicationHtml: Estimate the altitude and azimuth of the Moon, planets, or stars from a specific location.
kps.application: Estimate the altitude and azimuth of the Moon, planets, or stars from a specific location.

concepts.titleHtml: Optional Extension — At Issue: Ursa Major: How many stories over time and space? — p.314–315
concepts.title: Optional Extension — At Issue: Ursa Major: How many stories over time and space? — p.314–315

kps.title: KP1: Ursa Major across cultures
kps.coreHtml: The same star pattern has been interpreted differently across cultures and historical periods.
kps.core: The same star pattern has been interpreted differently across cultures and historical periods.
kps.applicationHtml: Compare Ursa Major stories from Greek, First Peoples, African, Arabic, Chinese, and Indian traditions.
kps.application: Compare Ursa Major stories from Greek, First Peoples, African, Arabic, Chinese, and Indian traditions.

kps.titleHtml: KP2: Big Dipper and cultural meaning
kps.title: KP2: Big Dipper and cultural meaning
kps.coreHtml: The Big Dipper can be understood as part of Ursa Major, a drinking gourd, a coffin, a celestial empire, or seven sages.
kps.core: The Big Dipper can be understood as part of Ursa Major, a drinking gourd, a coffin, a celestial empire, or seven sages.
kps.applicationHtml: Explain how culture shapes meaning in sky patterns.
kps.application: Explain how culture shapes meaning in sky patterns.

kps.title: KP3: Sky stories and navigation
kps.coreHtml: Star stories can preserve practical knowledge about direction, seasons, and survival.
kps.core: Star stories can preserve practical knowledge about direction, seasons, and survival.
kps.applicationHtml: Connect the Big Dipper and Polaris to navigation and cultural memory.
kps.application: Connect the Big Dipper and Polaris to navigation and cultural memory.

kps.title: KP4: Astronomy and shared knowledge
kps.coreHtml: Star names and sky traditions reflect contributions from many peoples, including astronomers of the Islamic world.
kps.core: Star names and sky traditions reflect contributions from many peoples, including astronomers of the Islamic world.
kps.applicationHtml: Recognize astronomy as a shared human knowledge system shaped by many cultures.
kps.application: Recognize astronomy as a shared human knowledge system shaped by many cultures.

concepts.titleHtml: Optional Investigation — Investigation 4-A: Using a Star Map — p.316–317
concepts.title: Optional Investigation — Investigation 4-A: Using a Star Map — p.316–317

kps.title: KP1: Star maps
kps.coreHtml: A star map shows constellations and bright stars visible at a certain time of year and from a specific hemisphere or latitude range.
kps.core: A star map shows constellations and bright stars visible at a certain time of year and from a specific hemisphere or latitude range.
kps.applicationHtml: Use a northern hemisphere star map to identify constellations visible from Canada.
kps.application: Use a northern hemisphere star map to identify constellations visible from Canada.

kps.titleHtml: KP2: Big Dipper as a guide
kps.title: KP2: Big Dipper as a guide
kps.coreHtml: The Big Dipper’s pointer stars can help locate Polaris and other constellations.
kps.core: The Big Dipper’s pointer stars can help locate Polaris and other constellations.
kps.applicationHtml: Use the Big Dipper to find the Little Dipper, Cassiopeia, Boötes, and Leo.
kps.application: Use the Big Dipper to find the Little Dipper, Cassiopeia, Boötes, and Leo.

kps.title: KP3: Seasonal constellations
kps.coreHtml: Different constellations are visible during different seasons.
kps.core: Different constellations are visible during different seasons.
kps.applicationHtml: Use a star map to identify constellations visible in each season.
kps.application: Use a star map to identify constellations visible in each season.

kps.title: KP4: Zodiac constellations
kps.coreHtml: Zodiac constellations are visible during specific months and are along the path where planets can appear.
kps.core: Zodiac constellations are visible during specific months and are along the path where planets can appear.
kps.applicationHtml: Locate zodiac constellations such as Leo, Virgo, Scorpius, Taurus, Gemini, and Cancer.
kps.application: Locate zodiac constellations such as Leo, Virgo, Scorpius, Taurus, Gemini, and Cancer.

concepts.titleHtml: Optional Investigation — Investigation 4-B: Modelling Motion in the Night Sky — p.318–319
concepts.title: Optional Investigation — Investigation 4-B: Modelling Motion in the Night Sky — p.318–319

kps.title: KP1: Planisphere
kps.coreHtml: A planisphere is a rotating star-map device used to predict visible constellations at a location and time.
kps.core: A planisphere is a rotating star-map device used to predict visible constellations at a location and time.
kps.applicationHtml: Use date and time settings to identify which constellations are visible.
kps.application: Use date and time settings to identify which constellations are visible.

kps.title: KP2: Apparent star motion
kps.coreHtml: Stars appear to move because Earth rotates on its axis and revolves around the Sun.
kps.core: Stars appear to move because Earth rotates on its axis and revolves around the Sun.
kps.applicationHtml: Explain why constellations appear to rotate around Polaris.
kps.application: Explain why constellations appear to rotate around Polaris.

kps.title: KP3: Predictable sky patterns
kps.coreHtml: Careful observation allows scientists to model and predict the positions of celestial objects.
kps.core: Careful observation allows scientists to model and predict the positions of celestial objects.
kps.applicationHtml: Use a model to compare visible constellations at different dates and times.
kps.application: Use a model to compare visible constellations at different dates and times.

kps.title: KP4: Model limitations
kps.coreHtml: A planisphere simplifies the sky and is designed for particular locations and latitude ranges.
kps.core: A planisphere simplifies the sky and is designed for particular locations and latitude ranges.
kps.applicationHtml: Evaluate when a planisphere is useful and when it may be inaccurate.
kps.application: Evaluate when a planisphere is useful and when it may be inaccurate.

chapters.titleHtml: Chapter 4.3: How has technology expanded our knowledge and understanding of the universe?
chapters.title: Chapter 4.3: How has technology expanded our knowledge and understanding of the universe?

concepts.titleHtml: 4.3 Concept 1: We have invented telescopes and other devices that extend and enhance our sense of sight — p.322–323
concepts.title: 4.3 Concept 1: We have invented telescopes and other devices that extend and enhance our sense of sight — p.322–323

kps.title: KP1: Limits of human vision
kps.coreHtml: Human eyes detect only visible light, so technology is needed to collect more light and detect other forms of radiation.
kps.core: Human eyes detect only visible light, so technology is needed to collect more light and detect other forms of radiation.
kps.applicationHtml: Explain why unaided-eye observations give only part of the information available from space.
kps.application: Explain why unaided-eye observations give only part of the information available from space.

kps.title: KP2: Optical telescopes
kps.coreHtml: Optical telescopes collect visible light over a large area and concentrate it to form a brighter image.
kps.core: Optical telescopes collect visible light over a large area and concentrate it to form a brighter image.
kps.applicationHtml: Explain how Galileo’s telescope allowed new observations such as Jupiter’s moons and Saturn’s rings.
kps.application: Explain how Galileo’s telescope allowed new observations such as Jupiter’s moons and Saturn’s rings.

kps.title: KP3: Electromagnetic radiation from space
kps.coreHtml: Visible light is only one form of electromagnetic radiation given off by stars and other objects in space.
kps.core: Visible light is only one form of electromagnetic radiation given off by stars and other objects in space.
kps.applicationHtml: Recognize that radio waves, infrared, ultraviolet, X-rays, and gamma rays can also carry information about space.
kps.application: Recognize that radio waves, infrared, ultraviolet, X-rays, and gamma rays can also carry information about space.

kps.title: KP4: Radio telescopes
kps.coreHtml: Radio telescopes detect radio waves, including long-wavelength radio waves that can pass through clouds.
kps.core: Radio telescopes detect radio waves, including long-wavelength radio waves that can pass through clouds.
kps.applicationHtml: Explain why radio telescopes can observe space on cloudy days and at night.
kps.application: Explain why radio telescopes can observe space on cloudy days and at night.

kps.title: KP5: Atmospheric absorption
kps.coreHtml: Earth’s atmosphere absorbs much of the radiation coming from space before it reaches the surface.
kps.core: Earth’s atmosphere absorbs much of the radiation coming from space before it reaches the surface.
kps.applicationHtml: Explain why some telescopes must be placed above the atmosphere.
kps.application: Explain why some telescopes must be placed above the atmosphere.

kps.title: KP6: Space telescopes
kps.coreHtml: Space telescopes observe radiation that is partly or fully blocked by Earth’s atmosphere.
kps.core: Space telescopes observe radiation that is partly or fully blocked by Earth’s atmosphere.
kps.applicationHtml: Connect Chandra, Spitzer, and Hubble to observations using X-ray, infrared, visible, or ultraviolet radiation.
kps.application: Connect Chandra, Spitzer, and Hubble to observations using X-ray, infrared, visible, or ultraviolet radiation.

kps.title: KP7: Multi-wavelength astronomy
kps.coreHtml: Viewing the same object in different wavelengths reveals different physical features.
kps.core: Viewing the same object in different wavelengths reveals different physical features.
kps.applicationHtml: Use Saturn images in visible, ultraviolet, infrared, and radio wavelengths to show how technology expands understanding.
kps.application: Use Saturn images in visible, ultraviolet, infrared, and radio wavelengths to show how technology expands understanding.

concepts.titleHtml: 4.3 Concept 2: We know that our Milky Way galaxy is just one of many billions of galaxies in the universe — p.326–332
concepts.title: 4.3 Concept 2: We know that our Milky Way galaxy is just one of many billions of galaxies in the universe — p.326–332

kps.title: KP1: Galaxy
kps.coreHtml: A galaxy is a collection of many billions of stars, plus gas and dust, held together by gravity.
kps.core: A galaxy is a collection of many billions of stars, plus gas and dust, held together by gravity.
kps.applicationHtml: Identify the Milky Way as the galaxy that contains the Sun and all stars visible in the night sky.
kps.application: Identify the Milky Way as the galaxy that contains the Sun and all stars visible in the night sky.

kps.titleHtml: KP2: Discovery of the Milky Way’s nature
kps.title: KP2: Discovery of the Milky Way’s nature
kps.coreHtml: Telescopes helped show that the hazy Milky Way band is made of individual stars.
kps.core: Telescopes helped show that the hazy Milky Way band is made of individual stars.
kps.applicationHtml: Connect Galileo, William Herschel, and Caroline Herschel to changing understanding of the Milky Way.
kps.application: Connect Galileo, William Herschel, and Caroline Herschel to changing understanding of the Milky Way.

kps.title: KP3: Galaxy formation and structure
kps.coreHtml: A galaxy forms when gravity causes a large, slowly spinning cloud of gas, dust, and stars to contract.
kps.core: A galaxy forms when gravity causes a large, slowly spinning cloud of gas, dust, and stars to contract.
kps.applicationHtml: Explain why gravity is central to the formation and structure of galaxies.
kps.application: Explain why gravity is central to the formation and structure of galaxies.

kps.title: KP4: Galaxy types
kps.coreHtml: Galaxies can be classified as elliptical, spiral, barred spiral, or irregular based on shape.
kps.core: Galaxies can be classified as elliptical, spiral, barred spiral, or irregular based on shape.
kps.applicationHtml: Compare galaxy types using features such as central bulge, spiral arms, gas, dust, and young stars.
kps.application: Compare galaxy types using features such as central bulge, spiral arms, gas, dust, and young stars.

kps.titleHtml: KP5: Observation angle and classification limits
kps.title: KP5: Observation angle and classification limits
kps.coreHtml: Galaxy classification can be difficult because galaxies may be blocked by gas, dust, other objects, or viewing angle.
kps.core: Galaxy classification can be difficult because galaxies may be blocked by gas, dust, other objects, or viewing angle.
kps.applicationHtml: Explain why the same galaxy may appear different depending on how it is observed.
kps.application: Explain why the same galaxy may appear different depending on how it is observed.

kps.titleHtml: KP6: Star clusters and Milky Way mapping
kps.title: KP6: Star clusters and Milky Way mapping
kps.coreHtml: Star clusters are groups of stars held together by gravity; globular cluster distribution helped show that the Sun is not at the centre of the Milky Way.
kps.core: Star clusters are groups of stars held together by gravity; globular cluster distribution helped show that the Sun is not at the centre of the Milky Way.
kps.applicationHtml: Use Harlow Shapley’s reasoning to explain how astronomers inferred our position in the galaxy.
kps.application: Use Harlow Shapley’s reasoning to explain how astronomers inferred our position in the galaxy.

kps.titleHtml: KP7: Local Group and larger structures
kps.title: KP7: Local Group and larger structures
kps.coreHtml: The Milky Way belongs to the Local Group, which belongs to the Virgo Supercluster, one of millions of superclusters.
kps.core: The Milky Way belongs to the Local Group, which belongs to the Virgo Supercluster, one of millions of superclusters.
kps.applicationHtml: Place the Milky Way in a larger cosmic structure that includes Andromeda and many other galaxies.
kps.application: Place the Milky Way in a larger cosmic structure that includes Andromeda and many other galaxies.

concepts.titleHtml: 4.3 Concept 3: There are vast distances separating stars and galaxies — p.334–337
concepts.title: 4.3 Concept 3: There are vast distances separating stars and galaxies — p.334–337

kps.title: KP1: Astronomical unit
kps.coreHtml: One astronomical unit, or \(1\,AU\), is the distance between Earth and the Sun, about \(150\,000\,000\,km\).
kps.core: One astronomical unit, or \(1\,AU\), is the distance between Earth and the Sun, about \(150\,000\,000\,km\).
kps.applicationHtml: Use \(AU\) for distances within the solar system.
kps.application: Use \(AU\) for distances within the solar system.

kps.title: KP2: Light-year
kps.coreHtml: A light-year is the distance light travels in one year, about \(9.46 \times 10^{12}\,km\).
kps.core: A light-year is the distance light travels in one year, about \(9.46 10 12 \,km\).
kps.applicationHtml: Use light-years for distances between stars and galaxies.
kps.application: Use light-years for distances between stars and galaxies.

kps.title: KP3: Speed of light
kps.coreHtml: Light travels through space at about \(300\,000\,km/s\).
kps.core: Light travels through space at about \(300\,000\,km/s\).
kps.applicationHtml: Connect the speed of light to the enormous scale of stellar and galactic distances.
kps.application: Connect the speed of light to the enormous scale of stellar and galactic distances.

kps.title: KP4: Looking back in time
kps.coreHtml: The farther away an object is, the longer its light takes to reach Earth.
kps.core: The farther away an object is, the longer its light takes to reach Earth.
kps.applicationHtml: Explain why observing Vega means seeing it as it was about 25 years ago and why the Sun appears about 8 minutes old.
kps.application: Explain why observing Vega means seeing it as it was about 25 years ago and why the Sun appears about 8 minutes old.

kps.title: KP5: Parallax
kps.coreHtml: Parallax is the apparent change in position of an object against a fixed background when viewed from two different lines of sight.
kps.core: Parallax is the apparent change in position of an object against a fixed background when viewed from two different lines of sight.
kps.applicationHtml: Use parallax to explain how astronomers estimate distances to nearby stars.
kps.application: Use parallax to explain how astronomers estimate distances to nearby stars.

kps.title: KP6: Triangulation and parallax limits
kps.coreHtml: Astronomers use Earth’s orbit as the known side of a triangle, but parallax becomes too imprecise for very distant stars.
kps.core: Astronomers use Earth’s orbit as the known side of a triangle, but parallax becomes too imprecise for very distant stars.
kps.applicationHtml: Explain why satellites such as HIPPARCOS and Gaia improve distance measurements for many stars.
kps.application: Explain why satellites such as HIPPARCOS and Gaia improve distance measurements for many stars.

concepts.titleHtml: 4.3 Concept 4: The properties of stars help us develop an understanding of their life cycles — p.338–347
concepts.title: 4.3 Concept 4: The properties of stars help us develop an understanding of their life cycles — p.338–347

kps.title: KP1: Star properties
kps.coreHtml: Important star properties include colour, temperature, luminosity, mass, and brightness.
kps.core: Important star properties include colour, temperature, luminosity, mass, and brightness.
kps.applicationHtml: Use observable properties to compare stars and infer their stage of development.
kps.application: Use observable properties to compare stars and infer their stage of development.

kps.title: KP2: Colour and temperature
kps.coreHtml: Star colour is related to surface temperature; blue stars are hotter and red stars are cooler.
kps.core: Star colour is related to surface temperature; blue stars are hotter and red stars are cooler.
kps.applicationHtml: Infer relative temperature from star colour.
kps.application: Infer relative temperature from star colour.

kps.title: KP3: Luminosity and brightness
kps.coreHtml: Luminosity is a star’s actual energy output, while apparent brightness depends on both luminosity and distance from Earth.
kps.core: Luminosity is a star’s actual energy output, while apparent brightness depends on both luminosity and distance from Earth.
kps.applicationHtml: Avoid assuming the brightest-looking star is always the most luminous.
kps.application: Avoid assuming the brightest-looking star is always the most luminous.

kps.title: KP4: Stellar mass
kps.coreHtml: Stellar mass is often compared with the Sun’s mass, called one solar mass.
kps.core: Stellar mass is often compared with the Sun’s mass, called one solar mass.
kps.applicationHtml: Use mass to predict how long a star remains active and what it may become.
kps.application: Use mass to predict how long a star remains active and what it may become.

kps.title: KP5: Hertzsprung-Russell diagram
kps.coreHtml: A Hertzsprung-Russell, or H-R, diagram compares star properties such as colour, temperature, luminosity, and absolute magnitude.
kps.core: A Hertzsprung-Russell, or H-R, diagram compares star properties such as colour, temperature, luminosity, and absolute magnitude.
kps.applicationHtml: Use an H-R diagram to identify patterns among stars.
kps.application: Use an H-R diagram to identify patterns among stars.

kps.title: KP6: Main sequence stars
kps.coreHtml: The main sequence is a diagonal band on the H-R diagram that contains about \(90\%\) of the stars visible from Earth.
kps.core: The main sequence is a diagonal band on the H-R diagram that contains about \(90\%\) of the stars visible from Earth.
kps.applicationHtml: Explain why most stars are classified as main sequence stars.
kps.application: Explain why most stars are classified as main sequence stars.

kps.titleHtml: KP7: Star life cycles and initial mass
kps.title: KP7: Star life cycles and initial mass
kps.coreHtml: A star’s life cycle depends mainly on its initial mass on the main sequence.
kps.core: A star’s life cycle depends mainly on its initial mass on the main sequence.
kps.applicationHtml: Compare low-mass, intermediate-mass, and high-mass star pathways.
kps.application: Compare low-mass, intermediate-mass, and high-mass star pathways.

kps.title: KP8: Final stages of stars
kps.coreHtml: Stars can end as white dwarfs, neutron stars, or black holes, depending on their mass and evolution.
kps.core: Stars can end as white dwarfs, neutron stars, or black holes, depending on their mass and evolution.
kps.applicationHtml: Connect red giants, supergiants, supernovas, neutron stars, and black holes to stellar life cycles.
kps.application: Connect red giants, supergiants, supernovas, neutron stars, and black holes to stellar life cycles.

kps.title: KP9: Supernovas and heavy elements
kps.coreHtml: High-mass stars can form heavier elements and eject them into space during supernovas.
kps.core: High-mass stars can form heavier elements and eject them into space during supernovas.
kps.applicationHtml: Explain how elements in planets and living things can come from older stars.
kps.application: Explain how elements in planets and living things can come from older stars.

concepts.titleHtml: Optional Extension — At Issue: Who has the MOST to lose? — p.324–325
concepts.title: Optional Extension — At Issue: Who has the MOST to lose? — p.324–325

kps.title: KP1: MOST satellite
kps.coreHtml: MOST, or Microvariability and Oscillation of Stars, was Canada’s first space telescope.
kps.core: MOST, or Microvariability and Oscillation of Stars, was Canada’s first space telescope.
kps.applicationHtml: Connect MOST to the study of small changes in the brightness of Sun-like stars.
kps.application: Connect MOST to the study of small changes in the brightness of Sun-like stars.

kps.title: KP2: Precision brightness measurements
kps.coreHtml: MOST could detect very tiny changes in star brightness and monitor the same star for weeks.
kps.core: MOST could detect very tiny changes in star brightness and monitor the same star for weeks.
kps.applicationHtml: Explain how long-term brightness data can reveal information about star life cycles.
kps.application: Explain how long-term brightness data can reveal information about star life cycles.

kps.title: KP3: Microsatellite design
kps.coreHtml: Microsatellites are smaller and less expensive than large space telescopes.
kps.core: Microsatellites are smaller and less expensive than large space telescopes.
kps.applicationHtml: Compare MOST with Hubble in terms of size, cost, and mission design.
kps.application: Compare MOST with Hubble in terms of size, cost, and mission design.

kps.title: KP4: Canadian space technology
kps.coreHtml: Canadian researchers, engineers, agencies, and companies contributed to MOST’s design, launch, and continued use.
kps.core: Canadian researchers, engineers, agencies, and companies contributed to MOST’s design, launch, and continued use.
kps.applicationHtml: Evaluate how smaller space technologies can still make important scientific contributions.
kps.application: Evaluate how smaller space technologies can still make important scientific contributions.

concepts.titleHtml: Optional Extension — Science at Work: Focus on Astronomy — p.333
concepts.title: Optional Extension — Science at Work: Focus on Astronomy — p.333

kps.title: KP1: Astronomy-related careers
kps.coreHtml: Astronomy connects with careers in science, engineering, data analysis, technology, and communication.
kps.core: Astronomy connects with careers in science, engineering, data analysis, technology, and communication.
kps.applicationHtml: Identify how astronomy knowledge can support work beyond telescope observation.
kps.application: Identify how astronomy knowledge can support work beyond telescope observation.

kps.title: KP2: Space weather and atmosphere
kps.coreHtml: Some atmospheric scientists monitor space weather such as solar radiation storms.
kps.core: Some atmospheric scientists monitor space weather such as solar radiation storms.
kps.applicationHtml: Explain why space weather alerts matter for satellites, power grids, and radio communication.
kps.application: Explain why space weather alerts matter for satellites, power grids, and radio communication.

kps.title: KP3: Space technology systems
kps.coreHtml: Space exploration depends on tools such as satellites, robotic arms, observatories, and communication systems.
kps.core: Space exploration depends on tools such as satellites, robotic arms, observatories, and communication systems.
kps.applicationHtml: Connect astronomy to engineering and technology used in space missions.
kps.application: Connect astronomy to engineering and technology used in space missions.

concepts.titleHtml: Optional Investigation — Investigation 4-C: Build an H-R Diagram — p.348
concepts.title: Optional Investigation — Investigation 4-C: Build an H-R Diagram — p.348

kps.title: KP1: H-R diagram construction
kps.coreHtml: An H-R diagram can be built by graphing spectral type against absolute magnitude.
kps.core: An H-R diagram can be built by graphing spectral type against absolute magnitude.
kps.applicationHtml: Use star data to identify the main sequence pattern.
kps.application: Use star data to identify the main sequence pattern.

kps.title: KP2: Spectral types
kps.coreHtml: Main sequence stars can be grouped by spectral types \(O, B, A, F, G, K,\) and \(M\), with number subdivisions.
kps.core: Main sequence stars can be grouped by spectral types \(O, B, A, F, G, K,\) and \(M\), with number subdivisions.
kps.applicationHtml: Use spectral type labels such as \(O5\), \(B2\), or \(G2\) to place stars on a graph.
kps.application: Use spectral type labels such as \(O5\), \(B2\), or \(G2\) to place stars on a graph.

kps.title: KP3: Absolute magnitude prediction
kps.coreHtml: A pattern on an H-R diagram can be used to predict the absolute magnitude of a main sequence star.
kps.core: A pattern on an H-R diagram can be used to predict the absolute magnitude of a main sequence star.
kps.applicationHtml: Estimate absolute magnitude for stars such as the Sun, Vega, or a \(B5\) star.
kps.application: Estimate absolute magnitude for stars such as the Sun, Vega, or a \(B5\) star.

kps.title: KP4: Graph-based reasoning
kps.coreHtml: Graphs can reveal relationships that are not obvious from a list of data.
kps.core: Graphs can reveal relationships that are not obvious from a list of data.
kps.applicationHtml: Use an H-R diagram to support claims about star properties and life cycles.
kps.application: Use an H-R diagram to support claims about star properties and life cycles.

concepts.titleHtml: Optional Extension — At Issue: How did Jocelyn Bell make one of the most important astronomy discoveries of the 20th century? — p.350–351
concepts.title: Optional Extension — At Issue: How did Jocelyn Bell make one of the most important astronomy discoveries of the 20th century? — p.350–351

kps.title: KP1: Neutron star hypothesis
kps.coreHtml: Before neutron stars were observed, some astronomers proposed that supernovas could produce extremely dense neutron stars.
kps.core: Before neutron stars were observed, some astronomers proposed that supernovas could produce extremely dense neutron stars.
kps.applicationHtml: Connect theoretical prediction to later astronomical evidence.
kps.application: Connect theoretical prediction to later astronomical evidence.

kps.title: KP2: Radio telescope data
kps.coreHtml: Jocelyn Bell helped build a radio telescope and analyzed large amounts of chart-paper data.
kps.core: Jocelyn Bell helped build a radio telescope and analyzed large amounts of chart-paper data.
kps.applicationHtml: Explain how careful data analysis can reveal unexpected patterns.
kps.application: Explain how careful data analysis can reveal unexpected patterns.

kps.title: KP3: Pulsars
kps.coreHtml: Pulsars are pulsing radio sources later linked to neutron stars.
kps.core: Pulsars are pulsing radio sources later linked to neutron stars.
kps.applicationHtml: Connect repeated radio signals to the discovery of a new class of astronomical objects.
kps.application: Connect repeated radio signals to the discovery of a new class of astronomical objects.

kps.title: KP4: Recognition in science
kps.coreHtml: Scientific discovery depends on observation, interpretation, collaboration, and fair recognition of contributions.
kps.core: Scientific discovery depends on observation, interpretation, collaboration, and fair recognition of contributions.
kps.applicationHtml: Evaluate why Jocelyn Bell’s role in the discovery of pulsars raises questions about fairness in science.
kps.application: Evaluate why Jocelyn Bell’s role in the discovery of pulsars raises questions about fairness in science.

chapters.titleHtml: Chapter 4.4: How do we use the big bang theory to explain what we know about the universe?
chapters.title: Chapter 4.4: How do we use the big bang theory to explain what we know about the universe?

concepts.titleHtml: 4.4 Concept 1: The big bang theory is based on two main sets of evidence: redshift and cosmic background radiation — p.354–358
concepts.title: 4.4 Concept 1: The big bang theory is based on two main sets of evidence: redshift and cosmic background radiation — p.354–358

kps.title: KP1: Big bang theory
kps.coreHtml: The big bang theory explains the origin of the universe, how it began, how it has changed, and how it is changing.
kps.core: The big bang theory explains the origin of the universe, how it began, how it has changed, and how it is changing.
kps.applicationHtml: Use the theory to connect evidence about expansion with the history of the universe.
kps.application: Use the theory to connect evidence about expansion with the history of the universe.

kps.title: KP2: Cosmology
kps.coreHtml: Cosmology is the study of the origin, structure, development, and possible future of the universe.
kps.core: Cosmology is the study of the origin, structure, development, and possible future of the universe.
kps.applicationHtml: Recognize the big bang theory as part of Western scientific cosmology.
kps.application: Recognize the big bang theory as part of Western scientific cosmology.

kps.title: KP3: Doppler effect
kps.coreHtml: The Doppler effect occurs when waves from a moving source are compressed or stretched.
kps.core: The Doppler effect occurs when waves from a moving source are compressed or stretched.
kps.applicationHtml: Use changes in light wavelengths to infer motion of stars and galaxies.
kps.application: Use changes in light wavelengths to infer motion of stars and galaxies.

kps.title: KP4: Redshift
kps.coreHtml: Redshift occurs when light from an object moving away is stretched toward longer, redder wavelengths.
kps.core: Redshift occurs when light from an object moving away is stretched toward longer, redder wavelengths.
kps.applicationHtml: Interpret redshifted galaxy spectra as evidence that galaxies are moving away from us.
kps.application: Interpret redshifted galaxy spectra as evidence that galaxies are moving away from us.

kps.title: KP5: Blueshift
kps.coreHtml: Blueshift occurs when light from an object moving toward an observer is shortened toward bluer wavelengths.
kps.core: Blueshift occurs when light from an object moving toward an observer is shortened toward bluer wavelengths.
kps.applicationHtml: Interpret blueshifted spectra as evidence that an object is moving toward the observer.
kps.application: Interpret blueshifted spectra as evidence that an object is moving toward the observer.

kps.title: KP6: Hubble’s law and expansion
kps.coreHtml: Hubble’s law shows that a galaxy’s speed away from Earth is proportional to its distance from Earth.
kps.core: Hubble’s law shows that a galaxy’s speed away from Earth is proportional to its distance from Earth.
kps.applicationHtml: Use redshift-distance patterns as evidence that the universe is expanding.
kps.application: Use redshift-distance patterns as evidence that the universe is expanding.

kps.title: KP7: Cosmic microwave background radiation
kps.coreHtml: Cosmic microwave background, or CMB, radiation is leftover radiation from the big bang that fills the universe.
kps.core: Cosmic microwave background, or CMB, radiation is leftover radiation from the big bang that fills the universe.
kps.applicationHtml: Use CMB radiation, including COBE and WMAP observations, as evidence that the universe was once much hotter and denser.
kps.application: Use CMB radiation, including COBE and WMAP observations, as evidence that the universe was once much hotter and denser.

concepts.titleHtml: 4.4 Concept 2: The big bang theory helps us describe how the components of the universe formed and have changed over time — p.360–361
concepts.title: 4.4 Concept 2: The big bang theory helps us describe how the components of the universe formed and have changed over time — p.360–361

kps.title: KP1: Looking back in time
kps.coreHtml: Because light has a finite speed, looking farther into space means looking farther back in time.
kps.core: Because light has a finite speed, looking farther into space means looking farther back in time.
kps.applicationHtml: Explain why distant galaxies show earlier stages of the universe.
kps.application: Explain why distant galaxies show earlier stages of the universe.

kps.title: KP2: Young universe and CMB
kps.coreHtml: The CMB shows the universe when it was about 380 000 years old and cool enough for photons to travel freely.
kps.core: The CMB shows the universe when it was about 380 000 years old and cool enough for photons to travel freely.
kps.applicationHtml: Connect CMB observations to an early stage of the universe.
kps.application: Connect CMB observations to an early stage of the universe.

kps.title: KP3: Early expansion and inflation
kps.coreHtml: Soon after the big bang, the universe expanded extremely rapidly during a period called inflation.
kps.core: Soon after the big bang, the universe expanded extremely rapidly during a period called inflation.
kps.applicationHtml: Describe early expansion as a major step in the universe’s development.
kps.application: Describe early expansion as a major step in the universe’s development.

kps.title: KP4: Cooling and particle formation
kps.coreHtml: As the universe expanded, it cooled, allowing energy, elementary particles, atoms, and later larger structures to form.
kps.core: As the universe expanded, it cooled, allowing energy, elementary particles, atoms, and later larger structures to form.
kps.applicationHtml: Use cooling to explain how the universe changed from energy-rich conditions to matter-filled conditions.
kps.application: Use cooling to explain how the universe changed from energy-rich conditions to matter-filled conditions.

kps.titleHtml: KP5: Formation of stars and galaxies
kps.title: KP5: Formation of stars and galaxies
kps.coreHtml: After the early universe cooled, the earliest stars and first galaxies began to form.
kps.core: After the early universe cooled, the earliest stars and first galaxies began to form.
kps.applicationHtml: Place star and galaxy formation within the timeline after the big bang.
kps.application: Place star and galaxy formation within the timeline after the big bang.

kps.title: KP6: Solar system formation
kps.coreHtml: The Sun and solar system formed much later than the earliest stars and galaxies.
kps.core: The Sun and solar system formed much later than the earliest stars and galaxies.
kps.applicationHtml: Recognize that Earth and the solar system are relatively late parts of cosmic history.
kps.application: Recognize that Earth and the solar system are relatively late parts of cosmic history.

kps.title: KP7: Technology and early-universe evidence
kps.coreHtml: Telescopes and particle accelerators help scientists study early galaxies and high-energy conditions similar to the early universe.
kps.core: Telescopes and particle accelerators help scientists study early galaxies and high-energy conditions similar to the early universe.
kps.applicationHtml: Connect the James Webb Space Telescope and CERN’s Large Hadron Collider to investigations of the universe’s early history.
kps.application: Connect the James Webb Space Telescope and CERN’s Large Hadron Collider to investigations of the universe’s early history.

concepts.titleHtml: 4.4 Concept 3: There is much about the universe that we still cannot explain — p.362–365
concepts.title: 4.4 Concept 3: There is much about the universe that we still cannot explain — p.362–365

kps.title: KP1: Continuing mysteries
kps.coreHtml: Even with modern technology, the universe still contains major mysteries about its structure, size, age, and evolution.
kps.core: Even with modern technology, the universe still contains major mysteries about its structure, size, age, and evolution.
kps.applicationHtml: Recognize that scientific explanations are refined as new evidence is collected.
kps.application: Recognize that scientific explanations are refined as new evidence is collected.

kps.title: KP2: Dark matter
kps.coreHtml: Dark matter is matter inferred from gravitational effects but not directly seen because it does not emit light.
kps.core: Dark matter is matter inferred from gravitational effects but not directly seen because it does not emit light.
kps.applicationHtml: Explain why astronomers infer dark matter from galaxy rotation and galaxy-cluster observations.
kps.application: Explain why astronomers infer dark matter from galaxy rotation and galaxy-cluster observations.

kps.title: KP3: Andromeda and missing mass
kps.coreHtml: Stars in the Andromeda galaxy move faster than predicted from visible matter alone.
kps.core: Stars in the Andromeda galaxy move faster than predicted from visible matter alone.
kps.applicationHtml: Use unexpected star speeds as evidence for additional unseen mass.
kps.application: Use unexpected star speeds as evidence for additional unseen mass.

kps.title: KP4: Dark matter halos
kps.coreHtml: Dark matter is thought to form large halos around galaxies.
kps.core: Dark matter is thought to form large halos around galaxies.
kps.applicationHtml: Explain why galaxy motion in groups and clusters suggests much more mass than visible matter shows.
kps.application: Explain why galaxy motion in groups and clusters suggests much more mass than visible matter shows.

kps.title: KP5: Dark energy
kps.coreHtml: Dark energy is a name for the unknown cause of the accelerating expansion of the universe.
kps.core: Dark energy is a name for the unknown cause of the accelerating expansion of the universe.
kps.applicationHtml: Use Type Ia supernova data to explain why scientists infer dark energy.
kps.application: Use Type Ia supernova data to explain why scientists infer dark energy.

kps.title: KP6: Accelerating expansion
kps.coreHtml: Astronomers expected expansion to slow because of gravity, but evidence shows expansion began accelerating about 7 billion years ago.
kps.core: Astronomers expected expansion to slow because of gravity, but evidence shows expansion began accelerating about 7 billion years ago.
kps.applicationHtml: Connect accelerating expansion to the need for a new explanatory idea.
kps.application: Connect accelerating expansion to the need for a new explanatory idea.

kps.titleHtml: KP7: Visible matter as a small fraction
kps.title: KP7: Visible matter as a small fraction
kps.coreHtml: Current estimates suggest the universe is mostly dark energy and dark matter, while visible matter makes up only a small fraction.
kps.core: Current estimates suggest the universe is mostly dark energy and dark matter, while visible matter makes up only a small fraction.
kps.applicationHtml: Explain why the visible universe is not the same as everything that exists.
kps.application: Explain why the visible universe is not the same as everything that exists.

concepts.titleHtml: Optional Extension — Make a Difference: Be Inspired — Dr. Stephen Hawking (1942–2018) — p.359
concepts.title: Optional Extension — Make a Difference: Be Inspired — Dr. Stephen Hawking (1942–2018) — p.359

kps.title: KP1: Hawking and black holes
kps.coreHtml: Stephen Hawking contributed ideas about black holes, matter, time, and space.
kps.core: Stephen Hawking contributed ideas about black holes, matter, time, and space.
kps.applicationHtml: Connect individual scientific work to changing ideas about the universe.
kps.application: Connect individual scientific work to changing ideas about the universe.

kps.title: KP2: Science and perseverance
kps.coreHtml: Hawking continued scientific work after being diagnosed with ALS.
kps.core: Hawking continued scientific work after being diagnosed with ALS.
kps.applicationHtml: Recognize persistence and curiosity as important scientific attitudes.
kps.application: Recognize persistence and curiosity as important scientific attitudes.

kps.title: KP3: Communicating complex ideas
kps.coreHtml: Hawking is remembered for both scientific insight and the ability to inspire public interest in the universe.
kps.core: Hawking is remembered for both scientific insight and the ability to inspire public interest in the universe.
kps.applicationHtml: Connect scientific discovery with public communication and imagination.
kps.application: Connect scientific discovery with public communication and imagination.

concepts.titleHtml: Optional Investigation — Investigation 4-D: The Age of the Universe — p.366–367
concepts.title: Optional Investigation — Investigation 4-D: The Age of the Universe — p.366–367

kps.title: KP1: Redshift-distance relationship
kps.coreHtml: A galaxy’s redshift is related to its distance from Earth.
kps.core: A galaxy’s redshift is related to its distance from Earth.
kps.applicationHtml: Use redshifted spectral lines to estimate galaxy velocity.
kps.application: Use redshifted spectral lines to estimate galaxy velocity.

kps.title: KP2: Hubble constant
kps.coreHtml: The slope of a graph of galaxy velocity versus galaxy distance gives the Hubble constant, \(H\).
kps.core: The slope of a graph of galaxy velocity versus galaxy distance gives the Hubble constant, \(H\).
kps.applicationHtml: Calculate \(H\) from a line of best fit.
kps.application: Calculate \(H\) from a line of best fit.

kps.titleHtml: KP3: Estimating the age of the universe
kps.title: KP3: Estimating the age of the universe
kps.coreHtml: The age of the universe can be estimated using \(\frac{10^{12}}{H}\), where \(H\) is the Hubble constant.
kps.core: The age of the universe can be estimated using \( 10 12 H \), where \(H\) is the Hubble constant.
kps.applicationHtml: Use graph data to estimate cosmic age.
kps.application: Use graph data to estimate cosmic age.

kps.title: KP4: Megaparsecs and velocity
kps.coreHtml: Astronomers use megaparsecs for large distances and velocity to describe motion toward or away from Earth.
kps.core: Astronomers use megaparsecs for large distances and velocity to describe motion toward or away from Earth.
kps.applicationHtml: Interpret units such as \(\text{km/s/Mpc}\) when working with Hubble’s law.
kps.application: Interpret units such as \( km/s/Mpc \) when working with Hubble’s law.

concepts.titleHtml: Optional Extension — Make a Difference: Take a Stand — Terraforming: Are we developers or caretakers? — p.368
concepts.title: Optional Extension — Make a Difference: Take a Stand — Terraforming: Are we developers or caretakers? — p.368

kps.title: KP1: Terraforming
kps.coreHtml: Terraforming is the proposed process of transforming an alien, lifeless world into a life-sustaining ecosystem.
kps.core: Terraforming is the proposed process of transforming an alien, lifeless world into a life-sustaining ecosystem.
kps.applicationHtml: Connect space science to questions about future colonization and planetary engineering.
kps.application: Connect space science to questions about future colonization and planetary engineering.

kps.title: KP2: Exoplanets and off-world possibilities
kps.coreHtml: Exoplanets are planets outside our solar system, expanding ideas about possible future human habitats.
kps.core: Exoplanets are planets outside our solar system, expanding ideas about possible future human habitats.
kps.applicationHtml: Compare solar-system colonization with broader possibilities beyond the solar system.
kps.application: Compare solar-system colonization with broader possibilities beyond the solar system.

kps.title: KP3: Ethical responsibility
kps.coreHtml: Terraforming raises questions about whether humans should act as developers or caretakers of other worlds.
kps.core: Terraforming raises questions about whether humans should act as developers or caretakers of other worlds.
kps.applicationHtml: Evaluate terraforming using environmental responsibility, technological possibility, and alternatives for solving Earth’s problems.
kps.application: Evaluate terraforming using environmental responsibility, technological possibility, and alternatives for solving Earth’s problems.

summaryHtml:
Unit Summary

- Total Chapters: 4

- Total Core Sections: 11

- Total Core Knowledge Points: 79

- Optional Extension / Investigation Sections: 11

- Optional Extension / Investigation Knowledge Points: 41

- Top 5 Core Formulas:

• Astronomical unit: \(1\,AU \approx 150\,000\,000\,km\)

• Light-year: \(1\,ly \approx 9.46 \times 10^{12}\,km\)

• Speed of light: \(c \approx 300\,000\,km/s\)

• Hubble’s law pattern: galaxy recession speed \(\propto\) galaxy distance, or \(v = Hd\)

• Hand-angle estimates: one finger \(\approx 1^\circ\), one fist \(\approx 10^\circ\), open hand span \(\approx 20^\circ\)

- Top 5 Common Error Areas:

• Treating the celestial sphere as a physical object instead of a model.

• Confusing constellations, asterisms, galaxies, star clusters, solar systems, and nebulae.

• Treating a light-year as a unit of time instead of a unit of distance.

• Treating the H-R diagram as a sky map instead of a graph of star properties.

• Confusing redshift, blueshift, dark matter, and dark energy.

- Suggested Learning Order:

• Start with perspective and meaning: universe, scale, culture, time, place, technology, and ways of knowing.

• Study unaided-eye astronomy: Sun, Moon, stars, visible planets, constellations, asterisms, Polaris, celestial sphere, ecliptic, azimuth, and altitude.

• Study astronomy technology: optical telescopes, radio telescopes, space telescopes, atmospheric absorption, and multi-wavelength astronomy.

• Study galaxies and distance: Milky Way, galaxy types, star clusters, Local Group, astronomical unit, light-year, speed of light, looking back in time, and parallax.

• Study stars: colour, temperature, luminosity, brightness, mass, H-R diagrams, main sequence, life cycles, supernovas, neutron stars, and black holes.

• Finish with cosmology: Doppler effect, redshift, Hubble’s law, cosmic microwave background radiation, early-universe timeline, dark matter, and dark energy.

- Suggested Review Order:

• Review sky-observation vocabulary first: universe, constellation, asterism, Polaris, celestial sphere, ecliptic, azimuth, and altitude.

• Practise distinguishing observation models from physical objects.

• Review distance units and scale: \(AU\), light-year, speed of light, parallax, and looking back in time.

• Review how technology expands evidence: optical, radio, infrared, ultraviolet, X-ray, gamma-ray, and space telescopes.

• Review star and galaxy organization: stars → star clusters → galaxies → galaxy groups → superclusters.

• Review big bang evidence and unresolved questions: redshift, Hubble’s law, CMB, dark matter, and dark energy.
